course_title: Writing and Publishing for Children and Young Adults

learning_outcomes.description: Critically evaluate and refine creative work through iterative drafting and workshopping, demonstrating autonomous decision-making and sophisticated revision strategies.
Integrate research, theory, and industry knowledge into creative practice, including awareness of contemporary MG/YA publishing contexts, ethical issues, and genre conventions.
Articulate and justify creative decisions using reflective, research-informed commentary aligned with Masters-level scholarly and professional practice.
Produce original, sustained creative work for the MG or YA market that demonstrates advanced control of essential craft techniques and audience positioning.

assessments.assessment_title: Workshopping, Annotated Draft Portfolio & Critical Reflective Essay
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires students to document and critically reflect on the development of an original creative work intended for the middle grade (MG) or young adult (YA) market. Students will submit annotated draft stages alongside a critical reflective essay that demonstrates advanced creative judgement, research-informed decision-making, and professional awareness of publishing contexts gained from workshopping and seminars.

Part A: Peer Review Workshops and Annotated Draft Portfolio

Requirements to pass the course hurdle:

  1. Students submit minimum of 3 (out of 4) substantial draft stages of the same creative work (e.g. MG chapter(s), YA short story, novella extract) by the deadline. Each draft must be 800-1000 words. You can re-submit the same draft so long as revisions are evident.
  2. Each submitted draft after the first one must include inline annotations or marginal commentary gained from lectures, workshopping, tutorial exercises and their own reading.
  3. Students review a minimum of 5 other student drafts from their group in writing and workshop those drafts in at least 2 in-person workshops.
  4. Students workshop their own draft with tutor observation in a minimum of 1 workshop session.


Part B: Critical Reflective Essay (1200-1400 words)

Part B cannot be completed without completing Part A

The essay must critically analyse the creative development process and explicitly reference moments of revision from the annotated drafts. Please always refer to Blackboard for full submission details and requirements.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Participate in workshops as outlined, having submitted annotated drafts in the weeks specified (via the PeerMark link). Submit critical reflection via Turnitin Link.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Whether you want to be a writer, a publisher, an editor, an agent, or a teacher, you'll need to understand the art of a good query letter and writing sample.

Your writing portfolio must include:

  1. a query letter (300-400 words - don't go outside of this range) to a specific publisher relevant to your MG or YA novel. You should research the publisher first to check relevance of your novel to their list.
  2. a 2000-2200 word writing sample (preferably opening scenes because they best give context, set up, hookiness, and immediate characterisation). This must be substantially the same story as the drafts submitted and workshopped during the course.
  3. your annotated drafts (minimum of 2)


If a submission doesn't adhere to the specified word range, the content of the submission must be assessed and the element of non-compliance marked through a separate criterion, and/or the assessment marked up to the specified word limit. Please always refer to Blackboard for full submission details and requirements.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Written assignments will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn. Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: From Morality Tales to Magic - The Birth of Children's & YA Literature

An overview of the Children's and Young Adult book category in Australia, its origins and the western literary canon.

We'll also be starting to research, plan and plot so you're ready to submit an early draft in Week 3.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 1: From Morality Tales to Magic - The Birth of Children's & YA Literature
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: From Morality Tales to Magic - The Birth of Children's & YA Literature An overview of the Children's and Young Adult book category in Australia, its origins and the western literary canon. We'll also be starting to research, plan and plot so you're ready to submit an early draft in Week 3.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Writing Openings that Pass the Library Browse Test - Guest speaker, Author Nick Earls

Leading Children's and Young Adult author Nick Earls will give insights to issues specific to the category. Be sure to have read After January.

We'll also be working on how to open the first scene of your novel.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 2: Writing Openings that Pass the Library Browse Test - Guest speaker, Author Nick Earls
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Writing Openings that Pass the Library Browse Test - Guest speaker, Author Nick Earls Leading Children's and Young Adult author Nick Earls will give insights to issues specific to the category. Be sure to have read After January. We'll also be working on how to open the first scene of your novel.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Research & Writing Week

NO SEMINAR THIS WEEK due to the Ekka public holiday.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Research & Writing Week NO SEMINAR THIS WEEK due to the Ekka public holiday.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Literary Taboos - Banned Books and Challenging Topics: Controversy in Children's and YA Literature

Banned books, trauma, voice appropriation, #metoo, own voices - we'll be discussing the issues and taboos surrounding YA and Children's Book Publishing. We'll also complete our first workshopping session.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 4: Literary Taboos - Banned Books and Challenging Topics: Controversy in Children's and YA Literature

We'll also complete our first workshopping session.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Coming of Age on the Page - MG and YA Fiction in Australian Education

Lecture about Middle Grade and YA Literature in the classroom and workshopping teacher's notes.

learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Coming of Age on the Page - MG and YA Fiction in Australian Education Lecture about Middle Grade and YA Literature in the classroom and workshopping teacher's notes.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: The Hero Nextdoor - How MG Novels Build Unforgettable Characters

With guest lecturer, Kate Foster, author of A River I Must Be followed by another workshopping session.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 7: The Hero Nextdoor - How MG Novels Build Unforgettable Characters
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: The Hero Nextdoor - How MG Novels Build Unforgettable Characters With guest lecturer, Kate Foster, author of A River I Must Be followed by another workshopping session.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: The Architecture of Images & Silence - Building Lasting Mental Pictures Through Words & Building a Query Letter

We'll discuss innovative writing techniques and we'll be workshopping how to write a query letter for your final assessment.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 8: The Architecture of Images & Silence - Building Lasting Mental Pictures Through Words & Building a Query Letter
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: The Architecture of Images & Silence - Building Lasting Mental Pictures Through Words & Building a Query Letter We'll discuss innovative writing techniques and we'll be workshopping how to write a query letter for your final assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Breaking the Rules Brilliantly - Taking Form and Voice to the Next Level

We'll discuss more innovative writing techniques and finish our final workshopping session.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: King's Birthday - Independent Writing Week

No seminar this week. Use this week to continue rewriting your query letter and your opening scenes for final submission.

Learning outcomes: L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 - Macro to Micro: The Comprehensive Novel Editing Hierarchy. Guest lecture from UQP children's and YA publisher Cathy Vallance.

After the guest lecture, we'll be discussing issues around employability.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: A day in the life of a Children's and YA Publisher with Wombat Books Publisher, Rochelle Stephens

Meet Rochelle - a powerhouse in the Children and YA Publishing world. We'll also be spending time editing our work and ironing out last minute issues.
